text: The video shows a large, complex incisional hernia in the patient's abdominal region, with visible protrusion through the abdominal wall.
The video shows a large, complex incisional hernia with minimal adhesions, primarily located in the upper portion of the hernia defect. Most of the adhesions are omental.
The surgical field reveals minimal adhesions, primarily located in the upper portion of the hernia defect.
The surgeon observes omental adhesions within the surgical field, noting their prevalence in the area.
The surgeon performs straightforward adhesiolysis, with no visible intestinal involvement in the adhesions.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, easily separating omental adhesions without encountering any intestinal involvement.

The surgeon identifies the liver densely adherent to the anterior abdominal wall in the subxiphoid region. Dissection is performed to develop the retromuscular space and incise the posterior rectus sheath, ensuring adequate cephaloid overlap for mesh placement.

The surgeon observes the liver densely adherent to the anterior abdominal wall in the subxiphoid region, preparing to dissect this area to develop the retromuscular space and incise the posterior rectus sheath for adequate cephaloid overlap of the mesh.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully separating omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall in the region of the incisional hernia.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis, carefully detaching the omentum and beginning to dissect part of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon plans to mobilize the omentum and begin dissecting the liver from the anterior abdominal wall, focusing on separating these structures from their adhesions.

The surgeon encounters an unexpected structure during dissection, initially uncertain of its identity. The colon is visually confirmed to be distant from the area, and the stomach is not in proximity. The surgeon examines the pylorus and the first portion of the duodenum, noting that the structure is not bowel-related.
The surgeon visually inspects the surgical field, noting that the stomach is not in proximity to the area being dissected.
The surgeon examines the pylorus and the first portion of the duodenum, noting the absence of bowel structures in the area despite the patient's history of a duodenal ulcer.
The surgeon continues the dissection, focusing on separating the omentum and part of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon continues the dissection process, focusing on separating the omental adhesions from the upper portion of the incisional hernia defect.
The surgeon places a Raytech sponge to collect the contents from the surgical site, ensuring no spillage or contamination occurs during the procedure.
The surgeon places a Raytech sponge to collect the contents from the surgical site, ensuring no spillage or contamination occurs during the procedure.
The surgeon continues to express a material resembling anchovy paste from the surgical site, carefully dissecting to prevent any spillage.
The surgeon continues to express a material resembling anchovy paste, carefully containing it within an EndoCatch bag and on a Raytech to prevent spillage.
The surgeon successfully contains the dissected material within an EndoCatch bag and on a Raytech sponge, preventing any spillage.
The surgeon retrieves the Raytech containing the collected contents from the surgical field, ensuring no spillage occurs.
The surgeon retrieves the Raytech containing the collected contents and cauterizes the liver bed to eliminate any residual material.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to cauterize the liver bed, ensuring no residual material remains in the area.
The surgeon removes the EndoCatch bag from the surgical field and replaces the instruments with clean ones, ensuring sterility for the next steps of the procedure.
The surgeon removes the EndoCatch bag from the surgical site and replaces the instruments with clean ones. A small portion of the liver is then dissected from the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon dissects a small portion of the liver from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating the liver tissue from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath in the upper portion of the abdomen, preparing for upper dissection to access preperitoneal and subside voidal spaces.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen to facilitate upper dissection, aiming to access preperitoneal and subside voidal spaces. The liver is visibly separated with ease during the dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection, resulting in the liver separating easily from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon begins the incision of the posterior rectus sheath in the upper abdomen, following the completion of previous dissection steps.
The surgeon begins the incision of the posterior rectus sheath in the upper portion of the abdomen, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath just off the midline, initiating the retromuscular dissection and development of the retromuscular space.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath just off the midline, initiating the retromuscular dissection and development of the retromuscular space. The incision is made near the liver edge, but the posterior rectus sheath is successfully incised.
The surgeon continues to incise the posterior rectus sheath near the liver edge, maintaining careful dissection to avoid compromising the liver tissue.
The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath along the planned dissection length, extending laterally to the linea semilunaris while carefully avoiding injury to visible neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath along the planned dissection length, corresponding to the hernia size. The retromuscular plane is developed laterally towards the linea semilunaris, with visible care taken to avoid injury to neurovascular bundles.
The camera focuses on the upper abdomen, showing the surgical field where previous incisions and dissections have been made.
In the upper abdomen, the surgeon navigates around the liver to continue incising the posterior rectus sheath, carefully maneuvering to avoid the liver while extending the dissection.
The surgeon continues to incise the posterior rectus sheath, maneuvering around the liver edge to expand the retromuscular space.
The surgeon dissects laterally from the initial incision point on the posterior rectus sheath, preparing to connect the dissection points on the contralateral side.
The surgeon dissects laterally on the contralateral side, carefully preserving a large neurovascular bundle visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates around a large neurovascular bundle while dissecting, ensuring its preservation during the procedure.
The surgeon continues the retromuscular dissection in the upper abdomen, ensuring the dissection extends to the desired area while leaving the liver undisturbed.
The surgeon ensures the retromuscular dissection is extended to the desired extent, focusing on the area behind the abdominal muscles.

The surgeon performs a crossover dissection in the suprapubic area, taking down the posterior rectus sheath below the arcuate line. The dissection progresses into the preperitoneal plane, approaching the space of Retzius.

The surgeon dissects the posterior rectus sheath in the suprapubic area, extending the dissection below the arcuate line into the preperitoneal plane, approaching the space of Retzius. The dissection is performed without encountering any inferior hernia components or inguinal hernias.
The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia, revealing the pubic bone below in the surgical field.

The surgeon incises the transversalis fascia to access the true pre-peritoneal plane, revealing the pubic bone below. The round ligament is visible but remains undivided as the dissection progresses laterally.

The surgeon attempts to enter the true pre-peritoneal plane, carefully tunneling laterally while the round ligament is visible. The round ligament remains intact, as the patient does not have inguinal hernias.
The surgeon creates a lateral tunnel, identifying the transversus abdominis muscle fibers. The muscle fibers are separated from the posterior elements, which are being preserved.
The surgeon creates a lateral tunnel, exposing the transversus abdominis muscle fibers. The muscle fibers are carefully separated from the posterior elements that are being preserved.
The surgeon begins the bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) approach, focusing on the lower abdominal region.
The surgeon begins a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR), separating the transversus abdominis muscle fibers while moving from right to left.
The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis muscle, moving from right to left, as part of the bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) procedure.

The surgeon separates the posterior components in the lower abdomen until reaching an awkward position, then transitions to the upper abdomen to perform a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon separates the posterior components until reaching an awkward position, then transitions to the upper abdomen to perform a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR). The preperitoneal plane is opened, and the posterior lamella of the internal oblique is visible. The transversus abdominis muscle fibers are incised, while the transversalis fascia remains intact in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon opens the preperitoneal plane by incising the posterior lamella of the internal oblique and the transversus abdominis muscle fibers, while leaving the transversalis fascia intact in the upper abdomen. The transversalis fascia is visible posterior to the muscle fibers.

The surgeon identifies the thin peritoneum posterior to the muscle fibers and begins to separate the transversus abdominis from the posterior elements, moving laterally to reinforce the posterior sheath.
The surgeon begins lateral separation of the transversus abdominis muscle from the posterior sheath, enhancing the structural integrity of the posterior elements.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the transversus abdominis (TA) muscle, proceeding from top to bottom, aiming to connect the upper and lower transversus abdominis release (TAR) dissections.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the transversus abdominis muscle from a top-down approach, carefully staying medial to the neurovascular bundles to avoid disrupting the semilunar line. The bulky transversus abdominis muscle transitions to an aponeurotic structure as the dissection progresses to meet the inferior dissection area.
The surgeon carefully dissects medial to the neurovascular bundles, avoiding disruption of the semilunar line to prevent lateral hernia defects and blowouts at the abdominal wall.
The surgeon navigates beyond the bulky muscular portion of the transversus abdominis, transitioning into the aponeurotic region, where it meets the inferior dissection area.
The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis muscle from the transversalis fascia, revealing the transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces.

The surgeon separates the transversus abdominis (TA) from the transversalis fascia, revealing the transition between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces. The transversalis fascia is visible below in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon transitions between the preperitoneal and pre-transversalis spaces, revealing the transversalis fascia below in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon continues dissection on the transversus abdominis muscle towards the lower abdomen, extending the lateral dissection posteriorly towards the psoas muscle.

The surgeon continues dissecting the transversus abdominis muscle towards the lower abdomen, extending the lateral dissection posteriorly towards the psoas muscle. The posterior rectus sheath is visible lying atop the viscera without tension. In the upper abdomen, the diaphragm fibers are seen meeting the transversus abdominis fibers. The liver, initially adherent to the posterior sheath, is successfully separated and moved down. The dissection progresses in the cephalad direction to ensure adequate overlap for mesh placement.
The posterior rectus sheath is positioned atop the viscera, visibly without any tension, indicating a smooth placement over the underlying structures.

The surgeon continues dissection in the upper abdomen, revealing diaphragm fibers meeting the transversus abdominis fibers. The liver, initially adherent to the posterior sheath, is successfully separated and lowered.

The surgeon continues opening the surgical field in the cephalad direction to ensure adequate overlap of the mesh. The dissection extends into the space of Retzius, although a complete dissection is not performed due to the absence of hernia defects. The mesh is positioned into the pelvis.

The surgeon dissects into the space of Retzius, noting the absence of hernia defects, and places mesh into the pelvis. A small hole in the peritoneum is closed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight fashion. Contralateral ports are placed as the right-sided TAR is completed, and preparations are made to perform the left-sided dissection.
The surgeon identifies a small hole in the peritoneum and closes it using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight pattern.
The surgeon places contralateral ports on the left side, mirroring the setup on the right side where the transversus abdominis release (TAR) has been completed.

The surgical team flips the boom and redocks the equipment to perform the left-sided dissection. The surgeon operates in the preperitoneal space, attempting a subxiphoid crossover despite minimal presence of the falciform ligament.

The surgeon remains in the preperitoneal space, performing a subxiphoid crossover. An incision is made in the peritoneum to maintain tissue integrity for potential visceral coverage. The posterior sheath is incised, and dissection is carried into the subxiphoid space above the liver, ensuring at least five centimeters of dissection for mesh overlap.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in the upper abdomen to preserve tissue for potential soft tissue visceral coverage. The incision of the posterior sheath on this side is initiated.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the subxiphoid space within the preperitoneal plane, extending the dissection at least five centimeters above the defect to ensure adequate mesh overlap.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the posterior rectus sheath, progressing down into the lower abdomen.

The surgeon continues dissection along the posterior rectus sheath into the lower abdomen, entering the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line to connect with the right-sided dissection and suprapubic crossover. Transversus abdominis fibers become visible as the retromuscular dissection extends laterally towards the semilunar line.
The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space below the arcuate line, connecting with the right-sided dissection and performing a suprapubic crossover dissection.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection, preparing to choose between a bottoms-up transversus abdominis release (TAR) approach or another technique, while completing the retromuscular dissection along the length of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins to visualize transversus abdominis muscle fibers while extending the retromuscular dissection laterally towards the semilunar line, carefully preserving neurovascular bundles.

The surgeon carefully preserves neurovascular bundles while dissecting around the port sites in the lower abdomen. TA fibers are visible in the upper abdomen. The retromuscular dissection continues, and the three port holes on this side will be closed with a three ovicle in a figure of eight fashion after completing the TAR procedure.

The surgeon identifies the transversus abdominis (TA) fibers in the upper abdomen, visible as distinct muscle fibers. Before proceeding with any release, the retromuscular dissection is continued, focusing on separating the muscle layers.

The surgeon dissects around the port sites, revealing three holes from the ports on one side. These holes will be closed with a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight pattern after completing the transversus abdominis release (TAR) on this side.
In the lower abdomen, the surgeon is in the preperitoneal space, separating the posterior elements from the lateral abdominal wall. The round ligament is visible on the patient's left side.

In the preperitoneal space, the surgeon identifies the round ligament on the patient's left side and separates the posterior elements from the lateral abdominal wall. The transversus abdominis muscle fibers are visible laterally as the surgeon creates a tunnel.
The surgeon exposes the transversus abdominis muscle fibers laterally and begins creating a tunnel through the retromuscular space.
The surgeon opts for a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR) approach, focusing on the upper abdominal region to continue the retromuscular dissection.

The surgeon opts for a top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR), revealing diaphragm fibers while ensuring they remain above to prevent a Morgagni hernia. The posterior lamella of the internal oblique is opened, and transversus abdominis muscle fibers are divided, allowing separation of components from the posterior elements in the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon identifies diaphragm fibers in the surgical field, ensuring they remain positioned above the operative area to prevent the creation of a iatrogenic Morgagni hernia.

The surgeon opens the posterior lamella of the internal oblique muscle, then divides the transversus abdominis muscle fibers to access the plane between the posterior elements and the lateral abdominal wall, extending the dissection laterally.

The surgeon continues the dissection laterally, keeping the diaphragm muscle fibers elevated. The posterior sheath is being separated from the raw transversus abdominis muscle. As the dissection progresses inferiorly, there is a transition from the pre-transversalis to the pre-peritoneal space. The aponeurotic portion of the transversus abdominis is opened near a port site, completing the dissection and connecting the upper and lower dissections.
The surgeon carefully removes the posterior sheath from the exposed transversus abdominis muscle, ensuring the diaphragm muscle fibers remain elevated above the surgical field.
The surgeon transitions from the pre-transversalis space to the pre-peritoneal space on the patient's left side, moving inferiorly, similar to the procedure performed on the right side.
The surgeon separates and opens the aponeurotic portion of the transversus abdominis muscle near one of the port sites, completing the dissection and connecting the upper and lower dissection planes.

The surgeon re-approximates the posterior rectus sheath using a 2-0 V-lock suture. The omentum, falciform ligament, and liver provide additional tissue for buttressing if needed. The posterior layer visibly comes together without tension.

The surgeon uses a two-oh V-lock suture to close the port site holes in the peritoneum on the left side, employing a figure-of-eight technique with three ovicle sutures. The falciform ligament is utilized to buttress the closure due to the weight of the liver, ensuring the posterior layer comes together without tension.

The surgeon uses parts of the falciform ligament to re-approximate both sides of the posterior layer, ensuring there are no holes. The heavy liver necessitates this approach, and various materials like omentum or vical mesh can be used for buttressing if needed.
The surgeon begins suturing from below, aiming to meet the sutures from above in the middle, as part of the closure process.
The surgeon ties the sutures together and proceeds to close the port site holes in the peritoneum using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a figure-of-eight pattern.

The surgeon prepares to close the defect created during the transversus abdominis release using a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture, which is visibly long and requires careful handling. The suture is tightened sequentially with multiple throws to ensure secure closure.

The surgeon closes a large defect using a number one non-absorbable V-lock suture, taking multiple bites through the linea alba and parts of the hernia sac to obliterate dead space. The suture is tightened sequentially, with the needle secured in the fascia to facilitate the process. The anterior rectus sheath edges are visible on each side of the rectus muscle.
The surgeon takes parts of the linea alba and the hernia sac to obliterate the dead space, ensuring the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon inserts the needle into the fascia to secure it while tightening the suture, preventing the need to search for the needle after running through the eighteen inches of suture.
The surgeon takes multiple bites of the anterior rectus sheath edges on each side of the rectus muscle, preparing to approximate the edges by starting from below and meeting the sutures from above.

The surgeon begins suturing from below, using multiple number one V-lock sutures to approximate the anterior rectus sheath. The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury to facilitate re-approximation. The surgeon carefully takes bites of the hernia sac, ensuring the thin skin above is not buttonholed or tented.

The surgeon uses multiple number one V-lock sutures to re-approximate the anterior rectus sheath, with the pneumoperitoneum decreased to eight millimeters of mercury for better visualization and control.
The surgeon works to transform a large, football-shaped defect into a smaller, ellipse-shaped defect, using sutures to progressively approximate the tissue edges.

The surgeon carefully takes shallow bites of the hernia sac, ensuring the thin skin above is not tented or dimpled, while aiming to obliterate the dead space and minimize seroma formation. A drain is left in place to manage fluid accumulation.

The surgeon carefully takes shallow bites of the fascia, avoiding deep bites into the muscle, to obliterate the space and minimize seroma formation. A drain is left in place. The suture is tightened sequentially after multiple throws.
The surgeon carefully takes bites of the fascia without involving much of the muscle, repeatedly throwing the suture until it can no longer be thrown, before proceeding to tighten it.
The surgeon sequentially tightens the suture down on the anterior rectus sheath, ensuring the needle remains visible for easy retrieval.

The surgeon secures the needle along the entire length of the incision to prevent loss, sewing back on itself using a V-lock suture. The surgeon reinforces areas with potentially weak fascia and accommodates a 30 by 30 centimeter piece of mesh in the measured space.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to sew along the entire length of the incision, reinforcing areas with potentially weak fascia. The suture is sewn back on itself to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon measures the defect space and accommodates a 30 by 30 centimeter piece of bioabsorbable mesh, Phasix, as the patient requested not to use permanent mesh.

The surgeon places a bioabsorbable mesh within the retromuscular space, ensuring it lays flat. A fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh, followed by the placement of a drain to complete the procedure.

The surgeon places a bioabsorbable mesh within the retromuscular space, ensuring it lays flat. A fibrin sealant is sprayed over the mesh, followed by the placement of a drain to complete the procedure.
